questions.question: Under the ITA 2025, old Section 54 (exemption for LTCG from residential house reinvested in residential house) maps to which new section?

options.text: Section 82
Section 85
Section 86
Section 84

questions.explanation: Old Section 54 maps to Section 82 — the residential house rollover exemption for individuals and HUFs. All conditions, time limits, and the Rs 10 crore cap (from Finance Act 2023) carry forward unchanged.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 82 is the residential house rollover (old 54). Section 85 is bonds (old 54EC). Section 86 is for LTCG from any asset other than a house reinvested in a house (old 54F). The mapping is sequential: 54→82, 54B→83, 54D→84, 54EC→85, 54F→86.

questions.question: Old Section 54EC (LTCG invested in specified bonds — NHAI/REC/PFC/IRFC) maps to which new section?

options.text: Section 83
Section 84
Section 85
Section 86

questions.explanation: Old Section 54EC maps to Section 85. The conditions are unchanged: LTCG from land or building invested in specified bonds within 6 months, max Rs 50 lakh per FY, 5-year lock-in, any assessee (not limited to individuals/HUFs).
questions.explanationWrong: Section 85 is the bonds exemption (old 54EC). Section 83 is agricultural land (old 54B). Section 84 is compulsory acquisition of industrial land (old 54D). The sequence: 54→82, 54B→83, 54D→84, 54EC→85.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct complete mapping for the exemption sections under the ITA 2025?
questions.explanation: The complete mapping: 54→82, 54B→83, 54D→84, 54EC→85, 54F→86, 54G→87, 54GA→88, 54H→89, 55→90, 55A→91. Ten sections renumbered sequentially. Note that 54EE and 54GB are absent — they were dropped.
questions.explanationWrong: The mapping follows the old section order: 54→82, 54B→83, 54D→84, 54EC→85, 54F→86, 54G→87, 54GA→88, 54H→89, 55→90, 55A→91. Notice 54EE and 54GB are missing — they were not carried forward.

questions.question: Old Section 54F (LTCG from any asset other than residential house, reinvested in residential house) maps to which new section?

options.text: Section 82
Section 86
Section 85
Section 87

questions.explanation: Old Section 54F maps to Section 86. This is the companion exemption to Section 82 (old 54): while 82 covers LTCG from selling a residential house reinvested in another house, 86 covers LTCG from selling any other long-term asset reinvested in a residential house.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 86 is old 54F. Section 82 is old 54. The distinction matters: 82 is house-to-house, 86 is any-asset-to-house. Both are for individuals/HUFs only, both have the Rs 10 crore cap (from FA 2023).

questions.question: Old Sections 55 (meaning of cost of acquisition/improvement) and 55A (reference to Valuation Officer) map to which new sections?

options.text: Section 90 and Section 91 respectively
Section 89 and Section 90 respectively
Section 88 and Section 89 respectively
Section 91 and Section 90 respectively

questions.explanation: 55→90 (cost of acquisition and improvement definitions) and 55A→91 (reference to Valuation Officer). These are the last two sections in the exemption chapter sequence. Both are renumbered only.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 90 is the cost definitions (old 55) and Section 91 is the Valuation Officer reference (old 55A). Section 89 is NRI extension of time (old 54H). The sequence: 54H→89, 55→90, 55A→91.

questions.question: Old Section 54EE (LTCG invested in specified fund for startups) was NOT carried forward into the 2025 Act. Why?

options.text: The fund was never notified — it was a dead letter provision from inception, and its sunset expired on 31 March 2021
The government opposes startup investment
The provision was merged into Section 82
The sunset date was extended but the provision was still dropped

questions.explanation: Section 54EE was a dead letter from day one — the specified fund was never notified by the government. The sunset date of 31 March 2021 passed without the provision ever being used. The 2025 Act simply chose not to re-enact what was never operative.
questions.explanationWrong: This is straightforward cleanup. The government never notified the fund required for 54EE to work. No one could ever use this exemption. With the sunset expired in 2021, re-enacting it in 2025 would have been pointless.

questions.question: Old Section 54GB (LTCG on residential property invested in eligible startup equity) was NOT carried forward. When did its sunset expire?

options.text: 31 March 2025
31 March 2021
31 March 2023
31 March 2026

questions.explanation: Section 54GB's sunset was 31 March 2025. With the 2025 Act effective from 1 April 2026, there is no transition gap — the exemption was already expired by the time the new Act takes effect. The 2025 Act simply chose not to re-enact an expired provision.
questions.explanationWrong: The sunset date of 31 March 2025 is key. Since the 2025 Act applies from 1 April 2026, the one-year gap means 54GB was already dead. It was not a 2025 Act policy decision — the provision simply ran its course.

questions.question: A client asks whether they can claim exemption under Section 54EE or 54GB for a transfer in FY 2026-27. What is your answer?

options.text: No — neither exemption exists in the 2025 Act; both were sunset-expired and not re-enacted
Yes — the saving clauses preserve these exemptions indefinitely
Only 54GB is available — its sunset was extended
They need to apply to CBDT for special approval

questions.explanation: Neither 54EE nor 54GB exists in the 2025 Act. 54EE was never operative (fund never notified, sunset 31 March 2021). 54GB expired 31 March 2025. For transfers in FY 2026-27, these exemptions are simply not available. No saving clause can help because the provisions were already expired under the old Act.
questions.explanationWrong: Both exemptions are gone. Saving clauses protect transactions that occurred while the old provisions were alive, but 54EE was never alive (fund never notified) and 54GB expired on 31 March 2025. For FY 2026-27, no avenue exists.

questions.question: How many exemption sections from the old Act (Sections 54-55A range) were NOT carried forward into the 2025 Act?

options.text: Two — Sections 54EE and 54GB
None — all exemptions continue
Four — Sections 54EE, 54GB, 54G, and 54GA
One — only Section 54EE

questions.explanation: Exactly two exemptions were dropped: 54EE (startup fund — never notified, sunset 2021) and 54GB (startup equity — sunset 2025). All other exemptions (54, 54B, 54D, 54EC, 54F, 54G, 54GA, 54H) continue with new section numbers. Sections 55 and 55A also continue.
questions.explanationWrong: Two and only two: 54EE and 54GB. Sections 54G (urban to non-urban shifting) and 54GA (shifting to SEZ) are alive and well as Sections 87 and 88 respectively. Don't confuse dropped with renumbered.

questions.question: What practical action should CAs take regarding Sections 54EE and 54GB?

options.text: Remove them from exemption checklists — they are not available for transfers under the 2025 Act
Keep them on checklists in case the government revives them
Replace them with new sections that have similar provisions
No action needed — clients rarely used these exemptions

questions.explanation: Clean up your checklists. These exemptions were already unavailable before the 2025 Act (54EE never worked, 54GB expired). The 2025 Act just makes it official. No replacement provisions exist — if the government wants to incentivise startup investment through capital gains exemptions, it would need a fresh amendment.
questions.explanationWrong: Remove and move on. These provisions were dead before the 2025 Act and are now formally absent. Keeping them on checklists wastes time and could confuse clients.

questions.question: What is the new feature of the Capital Gains Account Scheme (CGAS) under the 2025 Act?

options.text: The scheme now accepts electronic deposits — assessees can transfer funds electronically
The scheme has been abolished
The scheme now offers higher interest rates
The scheme is now managed by SEBI instead of banks

questions.explanation: The CGAS continues under the 2025 Act with one enhancement: it now accepts electronic deposits. Assessees can transfer funds electronically to their Capital Gains Account without physical deposit receipts. Account types (Type A savings, Type B term deposit) and designated banks remain unchanged.
questions.explanationWrong: CGAS is not abolished — it gets a digital upgrade. Electronic deposits are the new feature. The scheme structure (Type A and Type B accounts, designated banks) is unchanged. This is a modernisation of the deposit mechanism.

questions.question: Section 536(2)(h) of the ITA 2025 creates a claw-back provision. What specific scenario does it address?

options.text: An assessee claimed an exemption under the 1961 Act and violates the lock-in period after 1 April 2026
An assessee claims an exemption under the 2025 Act for the first time
An assessee's Capital Gains Account is closed before the lock-in expires
An assessee transfers property to a non-resident after claiming exemption

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(h) is a transition provision. It addresses the scenario where an exemption was claimed under the old Act (e.g., Section 54, 54B, 54EC, 54F) but the lock-in period violation occurs AFTER 1 April 2026. In that case, the claw-back is governed by the 2025 Act.
questions.explanationWrong: This is specifically about straddling lock-in periods. The exemption was claimed under the 1961 Act, but the violation happens under the 2025 Act regime. Section 536(2)(h) says the 2025 Act governs the claw-back — not the 1961 Act.

questions.question: Under Section 536(2)(h), if a client claimed Section 54 exemption in AY 2025-26 and sells the new house in FY 2027-28, which Act governs the claw-back?

options.text: The 2025 Act — the violation occurs after 1 April 2026
The 1961 Act — the original exemption was claimed under it
Both Acts apply — the more favourable one is chosen
Neither — there is no claw-back for old Act exemptions

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(h) is clear: if the lock-in violation occurs on or after 1 April 2026, the 2025 Act governs the claw-back. The original exemption was under old Section 54, but the sale of the new house in FY 2027-28 triggers claw-back under 2025 Act provisions.
questions.explanationWrong: The governing Act for the claw-back is determined by when the violation occurs, not when the exemption was claimed. Post-April 2026 violation = 2025 Act applies. This is a genuine transition provision that CAs need to flag for clients in active lock-in periods.

questions.question: The CGAS electronic deposit feature applies to exemptions claimed under which sections of the 2025 Act?

options.text: Sections 82 to 88 (all the rollover exemptions requiring reinvestment)
Only Section 82 (residential house)
Only Section 85 (bonds)
Sections 82 to 91 (all sections in the chapter)

questions.explanation: CGAS deposits secure exemptions under Sections 82-88 — all the rollover exemptions that require reinvestment within a specified time. Sections 89-91 are for NRI time extension, cost definitions, and valuation officer reference — they do not involve CGAS deposits.
questions.explanationWrong: CGAS is for rollover exemptions requiring actual reinvestment (Sections 82-88). Section 89 (NRI extension), Section 90 (cost definitions), and Section 91 (Valuation Officer) are not reinvestment exemptions and do not use CGAS.

questions.question: What practical step should CAs take for clients currently within lock-in periods for exemptions claimed under the 1961 Act?

options.text: Flag that post-April 2026 violations trigger claw-back under the 2025 Act per Section 536(2)(h)
Nothing — old Act exemptions are permanently safe
Re-file the exemption claim under the 2025 Act
Transfer the exempt asset to a trust to avoid the claw-back

questions.explanation: Review all clients currently in lock-in windows. If they claimed exemptions under old Sections 54/54B/54D/54EC/54F/54G/54GA and the lock-in extends beyond 1 April 2026, any violation after that date triggers claw-back under the 2025 Act. The rates and computation rules may differ from what the client expected.
questions.explanationWrong: Proactive client communication is essential. Clients who assumed the 1961 Act would govern any claw-back need to know that Section 536(2)(h) shifts the governing law to the 2025 Act for post-April 2026 violations.

questions.question: A client sells agricultural land and wants to claim exemption by reinvesting in agricultural land. Under the 2025 Act, which section applies?

options.text: Section 83 (old Section 54B)
Section 82 (old Section 54)
Section 84 (old Section 54D)
Section 87 (old Section 54G)

questions.explanation: Agricultural land exemption: Section 83 (old 54B). The land must have been used for agriculture for 2 years preceding the transfer, and reinvestment in new agricultural land must be within 2 years. Section 84 is for compulsory acquisition of industrial land. Section 87 is for urban-to-rural shifting.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 83 is the agricultural land rollover (old 54B). Don't confuse with Section 82 (residential house), Section 84 (compulsory acquisition of industrial land), or Section 87 (shifting of industrial undertaking).

questions.question: A client's industrial land is compulsorily acquired. They want to reinvest in land/building for re-establishing their undertaking. Which 2025 Act section applies?

options.text: Section 84 (old Section 54D)
Section 83 (old Section 54B)
Section 87 (old Section 54G)
Section 82 (old Section 54)

questions.explanation: Section 84 (old 54D) covers compulsory acquisition of industrial land/building with reinvestment in land/building for shifting or re-establishing the undertaking, within 3 years. Section 83 is agricultural land, Section 87 is voluntary shifting from urban to non-urban.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 84 = compulsory acquisition of industrial land (old 54D). Section 87 = voluntary urban-to-rural shift (old 54G). The distinction: 84 is for forced acquisition, 87 is for voluntary relocation.

questions.question: An individual sells listed shares (LTCG) and wants to invest the gain in a residential house. Which section of the 2025 Act provides the exemption?

options.text: Section 86 (old 54F) — LTCG from any asset other than residential house, reinvested in residential house
Section 82 (old 54) — residential house to residential house
Section 85 (old 54EC) — bonds exemption
No exemption is available for listed shares

questions.explanation: Section 86 (old 54F) covers LTCG from any long-term capital asset other than a residential house, reinvested in a residential house. Since listed shares are not a residential house, Section 86 applies. Section 82 applies only when the transferred asset itself is a residential house.
questions.explanationWrong: The 82 vs 86 distinction is critical. Section 82: house sold → house bought. Section 86: non-house asset sold → house bought. Listed shares = non-house, so Section 86. Both apply to individuals/HUFs only.

questions.question: Old Section 54G (shifting of industrial undertaking from urban to non-urban area) and 54GA (shifting to SEZ) map to which new sections?

options.text: Section 87 and Section 88 respectively
Section 88 and Section 87 respectively
Section 84 and Section 85 respectively
Both merged into a single Section 87

questions.explanation: 54G→87 (urban to non-urban shifting) and 54GA→88 (shifting to SEZ). Both are renumbered without change. The time limits (1 year before / 3 years after) continue.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 87 is urban-to-rural (old 54G) and Section 88 is shifting-to-SEZ (old 54GA). They remain separate sections. Section 84 is compulsory acquisition (old 54D), Section 85 is bonds (old 54EC).

questions.question: Old Section 54H (extension of time for NRI to acquire new asset) maps to which new section?

options.text: Section 89
Section 88
Section 90
Section 91

questions.explanation: Old Section 54H maps to Section 89 — extension of time for NRIs to acquire a new asset to claim exemption. Renumbered only. Section 88 is SEZ shifting (old 54GA), Section 90 is cost definitions (old 55), Section 91 is Valuation Officer (old 55A).
questions.explanationWrong: Section 89 = NRI time extension (old 54H). This completes the exemption sequence: 54G→87, 54GA→88, 54H→89, 55→90, 55A→91.

questions.question: A client sells property in FY 2026-27 and wants to claim Section 82 exemption but has not yet purchased a new house. They need to deposit funds in a Capital Gains Account before the return-filing due date. Can they make this deposit electronically?

options.text: Yes — the 2025 Act enables electronic deposits to CGAS
No — only physical deposits are accepted
Only if the deposit exceeds Rs 50 lakh
Electronic deposits are only available for NRIs

questions.explanation: Under the 2025 Act, CGAS accepts electronic deposits. The client can transfer funds electronically to their Capital Gains Account at a designated bank before the return-filing due date to secure the Section 82 exemption.
questions.explanationWrong: Electronic deposits are now available to all assessees, not just NRIs. There is no minimum threshold. This is a straightforward modernisation — physical deposits are still accepted but electronic is now an option too.

questions.question: A client claimed Section 54EC exemption (bonds) in AY 2024-25. The 5-year lock-in extends to FY 2028-29. If they redeem the bonds early in FY 2027-28, which Act governs the claw-back?

options.text: The 2025 Act — the violation occurs after 1 April 2026, triggering Section 536(2)(h)
The 1961 Act — the exemption was claimed under it
No claw-back applies — bonds redeemed early are simply taxed as income
SEBI regulations govern the claw-back, not the Income Tax Act

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(h) applies: the exemption was claimed under old Section 54EC, but the lock-in violation (early redemption in FY 2027-28) occurs after 1 April 2026. The claw-back is governed by the 2025 Act. The original capital gains become taxable under 2025 Act computation and rates.
questions.explanationWrong: The violation date controls the governing Act. FY 2027-28 is post-April 2026, so the 2025 Act applies via Section 536(2)(h). The 1961 Act governed the original exemption claim, but the claw-back event falls under the 2025 Act.

questions.question: Which of the following clients does NOT need to be flagged for Section 536(2)(h) claw-back risk?

options.text: A client whose Section 54EC lock-in expired in March 2026 — lock-in completed before the 2025 Act takes effect
A client whose Section 54 lock-in extends to FY 2027-28
A client whose Section 54F lock-in extends to FY 2028-29
A client whose Section 54B reinvestment is within its 2-year window extending to FY 2026-27

questions.explanation: If the lock-in is completed before 1 April 2026, there is no straddling risk. Section 536(2)(h) only applies when the violation occurs on or after 1 April 2026. The client whose lock-in expired in March 2026 is safe — no post-April 2026 violation is possible.
questions.explanationWrong: Section 536(2)(h) only matters when the lock-in extends into the 2025 Act period (post-April 2026). If the lock-in is fully completed before April 2026, there is nothing to claw back under the new Act.

questions.question: Under CGAS, what are the two account types that continue under the 2025 Act?

options.text: Type A (savings) and Type B (term deposit)
Type A (current) and Type B (fixed deposit)
Individual account and corporate account
Short-term account and long-term account

questions.explanation: CGAS continues with Type A (savings account — for funds needed at short notice for purchase) and Type B (term deposit — for funds parked longer, e.g., during construction). Both types are available at designated banks. The electronic deposit option applies to both.
questions.explanationWrong: Type A = savings, Type B = term deposit. These are the same account types from the original 1988 scheme. The 2025 Act does not change the account structure — only adds the electronic deposit channel.

questions.question: A client claimed Section 54 exemption in AY 2026-27 (transfer in FY 2025-26 under the 1961 Act). They have a 3-year lock-in on the new house ending in FY 2028-29. If they sell the house in FY 2028-29, the claw-back computation uses:

options.text: The 2025 Act's provisions — rates and computation rules as per the new Act
The 1961 Act's provisions — the original exemption was under old Section 54
The more favourable of the two Acts
No computation is needed — selling within the lock-in is penalty-free

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(h) directs that post-April 2026 violations trigger claw-back under the 2025 Act. The computation uses 2025 Act section numbers, and the rates applicable at the time of the claw-back event apply. This may differ from what the client expected when claiming the original exemption.
questions.explanationWrong: The 2025 Act governs — period. There is no 'more favourable' choice. Section 536(2)(h) is explicit: if the violation is post-April 2026, it is the 2025 Act's rules that apply. Alert clients about this before they make decisions about exempt assets.

questions.question: Under Section 82 (old 54), the time limit for purchasing a new residential house to claim exemption is:

options.text: 1 year before or 2 years after transfer (purchase); 3 years after (construction)
6 months after transfer only
3 years before or 5 years after transfer
The time limit was removed by the 2025 Act

questions.explanation: The time limits are unchanged: 1 year before or 2 years after the transfer date for purchase, or 3 years after for construction. These are the same limits that existed under old Section 54. The 2025 Act does not modify any time limit.
questions.explanationWrong: All time limits are carried forward from old Section 54 without change. 1 year before / 2 years after for purchase, 3 years for construction. If the client cannot complete within these periods, they must deposit in CGAS before the return-filing due date.

questions.question: The Rs 10 crore cap on exemptions under Sections 82 and 86 was introduced by:

options.text: Finance Act 2023 — it is carried forward into the 2025 Act, not a 2025 Act change
The Income Tax Act 2025 as a new provision
Finance Act 2024
There is no Rs 10 crore cap

questions.explanation: The Rs 10 crore cap on Sections 54 and 54F (now 82 and 86) was introduced by Finance Act 2023 effective AY 2024-25. The 2025 Act incorporates this into the base statute — it is NOT a 2025 Act innovation. This is a Finance Act change carried forward.
questions.explanationWrong: This is an important attribution question. The cap was Finance Act 2023, not the 2025 Act. The 2025 Act merely carries it forward as part of the base statute. Do not credit the 2025 Act with changes made by earlier Finance Acts.

questions.question: Under Section 85 (old 54EC), the maximum investment in specified bonds per financial year is:

options.text: Rs 50 lakh — unchanged from the old Act
Rs 10 crore — same as the house purchase cap
Rs 1 crore — increased by the 2025 Act
No limit under the 2025 Act

questions.explanation: Rs 50 lakh per FY, with a 5-year lock-in. This limit is unchanged — Section 85 is a pure renumbering of old Section 54EC. Any assessee (not just individuals/HUFs) can claim this exemption for LTCG from land or building.
questions.explanationWrong: The Rs 50 lakh per FY limit and 5-year lock-in are unchanged. Section 85 = old 54EC, no modifications. The Rs 10 crore cap applies to Sections 82 and 86 (house purchase exemptions), not to Section 85 (bonds).

questions.question: Section 82 (old 54) includes a two-house option for LTCG up to Rs 2 crore. Has this been changed by the 2025 Act?

options.text: No — the two-house option (once in a lifetime, LTCG up to Rs 2 crore) continues unchanged
Yes — the limit was increased to Rs 5 crore
Yes — the two-house option was removed
Yes — it is now available for every transfer, not once in a lifetime

questions.explanation: The two-house option continues: individuals/HUFs with LTCG up to Rs 2 crore can purchase two residential houses instead of one, once in a lifetime. This was introduced by Finance Act 2019 and is carried forward unchanged.
questions.explanationWrong: No change. The two-house option remains once-in-a-lifetime with the Rs 2 crore cap. The 2025 Act preserves this exactly as it was.

questions.question: Under Section 86 (old 54F), a key condition is that on the date of transfer, the assessee must not own:

options.text: More than one residential house (other than the new house being purchased)
Any residential house at all
More than three residential houses
Any property valued above Rs 50 lakh

questions.explanation: Section 86 (old 54F) requires that on the date of transfer, the assessee must not own more than one residential house (other than the new house). If they own two or more houses already, the exemption is not available. This condition is unchanged.
questions.explanationWrong: The condition is 'not more than one other house.' Owning zero or one house is fine. Owning two or more disqualifies you. This has always been the rule under 54F and continues under Section 86.

questions.question: It's March 2028. A client who claimed Section 54 exemption in AY 2025-26 (buying a house within the 3-year lock-in) wants to sell the new house. They ask: 'Will the claw-back be under the old Act since I claimed the exemption under it?' Your answer:

options.text: No — selling in FY 2027-28 (post-April 2026) means the claw-back is governed by the 2025 Act under Section 536(2)(h)
Yes — the 1961 Act governs the entire exemption cycle
There is no claw-back — the 2025 Act repealed all claw-back provisions
The client can choose which Act applies

questions.explanation: Section 536(2)(h) is explicit: post-April 2026 lock-in violations are governed by the 2025 Act. The client's expectation that the 1961 Act would govern is incorrect. The original exemption claim was under the old Act, but the enforcement event falls under the new one.
questions.explanationWrong: No choice, no old-Act fallback. Section 536(2)(h) overrides the assumption that the original claiming Act governs the entire cycle. Post-April 2026, the 2025 Act takes over for claw-back purposes.

questions.question: A client sells their only residential house in FY 2027-28 and wants to invest the LTCG in both a new house (Section 82) and specified bonds (Section 85). Is this possible?

options.text: Yes — Sections 82 and 85 are not mutually exclusive; the client can split the LTCG across both exemptions
No — the client must choose one exemption only
Only if the total exemption does not exceed Rs 10 crore
Only NRIs can claim both exemptions simultaneously

questions.explanation: Sections 82 and 85 are not mutually exclusive. A client can invest part of the LTCG in a new house (Section 82) and up to Rs 50 lakh in specified bonds (Section 85). This dual-exemption strategy has been available since the 1961 Act and continues.
questions.explanationWrong: No mutual exclusivity. The client can claim both Section 82 (house) and Section 85 (bonds) for the same LTCG from a residential house, subject to each section's individual conditions and limits.

questions.question: A colleague drafts an exemption claim under 'Section 54F of the Income Tax Act' for a transfer in FY 2027-28. What needs to be corrected?

options.text: The section reference — it should be Section 86 of the ITA 2025; the exemption itself is unchanged
Nothing — Section 54F continues to apply
The exemption no longer exists
It should be Section 82, not Section 54F or 86

questions.explanation: For transfers in FY 2027-28 (under the 2025 Act), the correct citation is Section 86. Old Section 54F is the 1961 Act reference. The exemption conditions are identical — only the section number has changed. Section 82 is for house-to-house (old 54), not asset-to-house (old 54F).
questions.explanationWrong: Pure citation error. The exemption is alive as Section 86. Section 82 would be wrong too — that is the house-to-house exemption (old 54). For non-house asset to house, it is Section 86 (old 54F).

questions.question: A client in 2029 asks about claiming exemption under Section 54GB for investing in a startup. Your response:

options.text: Section 54GB was not carried forward into the 2025 Act — it expired on 31 March 2025; no such exemption exists
The exemption is available under a new section number
DPIIT can grant the exemption by notification
The client should file under the 1961 Act using saving clauses

questions.explanation: Section 54GB expired on 31 March 2025 and was not re-enacted. For a 2029 transfer, no equivalent exemption exists in the 2025 Act. If the government wants to revive this, it would need a fresh legislative amendment.
questions.explanationWrong: 54GB is gone with no replacement. Saving clauses only protect transactions that occurred while the provision was alive. A 2029 transfer cannot benefit from a provision that expired in 2025.

questions.question: A client wants to deposit Rs 2 crore in CGAS electronically for a Section 82 exemption claim. Is there any limitation on the electronic deposit amount?

options.text: No specific limitation on the electronic deposit amount — the Rs 10 crore cap applies to the exemption itself, not the deposit method
Electronic deposits are capped at Rs 50 lakh
Electronic deposits are only available for amounts below Rs 1 crore
Electronic deposits are not available for amounts above Rs 10 lakh

questions.explanation: The electronic deposit facility has no separate amount cap. The Rs 10 crore cap (from FA 2023) applies to the total exemption under Section 82, not to the mode of deposit. A client can deposit Rs 2 crore electronically with no issue.
questions.explanationWrong: No amount restriction on electronic deposits. The CGAS just gained a new channel (electronic) alongside physical deposits. The exemption caps (Rs 10 crore for Sections 82/86, Rs 50 lakh for Section 85) apply regardless of deposit method.

questions.question: A CA firm is updating its exemption checklist for the 2025 Act. How many sections should the checklist contain, compared to the 1961 Act?

options.text: Two fewer — 54EE and 54GB are dropped; the remaining 10 sections are renumbered
The same number — all exemptions continue with new numbers
Five fewer — several exemptions were consolidated
Two more — the 2025 Act adds new exemptions

questions.explanation: The 1961 Act had 12 sections in the 54-55A range (including 54EE and 54GB). The 2025 Act has 10 (Sections 82-91). Two were dropped (54EE, 54GB). No new exemptions were added. The checklist shrinks by two items.
questions.explanationWrong: Exactly two fewer. Nothing was consolidated, nothing was added. 54EE (never operative) and 54GB (sunset expired) are simply gone. Update your checklist to reflect 10 sections, not 12.

questions.question: A client asks: 'Did the 2025 Act change any exemption conditions — time limits, caps, or lock-in periods?' Your answer:

options.text: No — all conditions, time limits, caps, and lock-in periods are unchanged by the 2025 Act; earlier Finance Act changes are merely carried forward
Yes — time limits were extended for all exemptions
Yes — the Rs 10 crore cap was introduced by the 2025 Act
Yes — lock-in periods were shortened

questions.explanation: The 2025 Act does not modify any exemption condition. Time limits, caps, and lock-in periods are all carried forward from the 1961 Act as amended. The Rs 10 crore cap was Finance Act 2023. The 2025 Act's changes are: two exemptions dropped, CGAS goes digital, and Section 536(2)(h) claw-back transition.
questions.explanationWrong: Zero exemption conditions changed by the 2025 Act. The Rs 10 crore cap was FA 2023. The only 2025 Act contributions are: (1) dropping 54EE and 54GB, (2) CGAS electronic deposits, (3) Section 536(2)(h) claw-back for straddling lock-ins.

questions.question: Identify the four genuine 2025 Act changes in Unit 7.3 (exemptions chapter):

options.text: 54EE dropped, 54GB dropped, CGAS electronic deposits, Section 536(2)(h) claw-back
54EE dropped, 54GB dropped, Rs 10 crore cap introduced, indexation removed
All exemptions redesigned, CGAS abolished, new startup exemption, rates changed
Only the section renumbering — no substantive changes

questions.explanation: Four genuine 2025 Act changes: (1) Section 54EE not re-enacted, (2) Section 54GB not re-enacted, (3) CGAS now accepts electronic deposits, (4) Section 536(2)(h) creates a claw-back for post-April 2026 lock-in violations on old-Act exemptions. Everything else is renumbering or prior Finance Act changes carried forward.
questions.explanationWrong: The Rs 10 crore cap was Finance Act 2023, not the 2025 Act. Indexation removal was Finance Act 2024. The four genuine 2025 Act items are: two dropped exemptions, CGAS digital upgrade, and the Section 536(2)(h) transition claw-back.

questions.question: A client with an ongoing Section 54B (now Section 83) exemption claim for agricultural land asks whether the 2-year reinvestment window changed under the 2025 Act. Your answer:

options.text: No — the 2-year window for purchasing new agricultural land is unchanged; Section 83 is a pure renumbering of 54B
Yes — the window was extended to 3 years
Yes — agricultural land exemption was removed entirely
The 2025 Act requires reinvestment within 1 year

questions.explanation: Section 83 = old 54B, no changes. The 2-year reinvestment window continues. The agricultural land condition (used for agriculture for 2 years preceding transfer) also continues. This is pure renumbering.
questions.explanationWrong: No change to the 2-year window. Section 83 carries forward every condition from old Section 54B without modification. The 2025 Act does not touch agricultural land exemption conditions.

questions.question: Section 90 (old 55) defines cost of acquisition. For an asset acquired before 1 April 2001, can the assessee substitute FMV as on 1 April 2001?

options.text: Yes — this rule continues unchanged under Section 90
No — the 2025 Act removed the FMV substitution option
Only if the asset is immovable property
Only if the asset was acquired by purchase (not by gift or inheritance)

questions.explanation: Section 90 (old 55) continues the FMV substitution rule: for assets acquired before 1 April 2001, the assessee can choose between actual cost and FMV as on 1 April 2001. This applies to all capital assets. Renumbered only.
questions.explanationWrong: The FMV substitution for pre-2001 assets is a long-standing rule preserved in Section 90. It applies to all assets, whether acquired by purchase, gift, or inheritance. No change by the 2025 Act.

questions.question: Quick match: Section 88 of the ITA 2025 corresponds to which old section and what does it cover?

options.text: Old Section 54GA — exemption for shifting industrial undertaking to SEZ
Old Section 54G — exemption for shifting from urban to non-urban area
Old Section 54H — NRI extension of time
Old Section 54EC — bonds exemption

questions.explanation: Section 88 = old 54GA = shifting to SEZ. Section 87 = old 54G = urban to non-urban. Don't confuse the two G sections: 54G (general shifting, now 87) and 54GA (SEZ shifting, now 88).
questions.explanationWrong: Section 88 is SEZ shifting (old 54GA). Section 87 is general urban-to-rural shifting (old 54G). The A suffix in 54GA distinguishes the SEZ-specific provision from the general one.

questions.question: A colleague says: 'The 2025 Act introduced the Rs 10 crore cap on house purchase exemptions.' Is this correct?

options.text: No — the Rs 10 crore cap was introduced by Finance Act 2023; the 2025 Act merely carries it forward
Yes — it is a new 2025 Act provision
Partially — the 2025 Act increased it from Rs 5 crore to Rs 10 crore
The cap applies only under Section 82, not Section 86

questions.explanation: The Rs 10 crore cap was Finance Act 2023, effective AY 2024-25. It applies to both Sections 82 and 86 (old 54 and 54F). The 2025 Act incorporates this as part of the base statute but did not create it. Attribution matters.
questions.explanationWrong: This is a Finance Act 2023 change, not a 2025 Act change. The 2025 Act codifies it into the base statute. The cap applies to both house-to-house (Section 82) and other-asset-to-house (Section 86) exemptions.

questions.question: A firm is migrating exemption templates. They need to replace old section numbers with new ones and remove two exemptions. Which two must be removed?

options.text: 54EE (never operative — fund never notified) and 54GB (sunset expired 31 March 2025)
54G (urban shifting) and 54GA (SEZ shifting)
54D (compulsory acquisition) and 54H (NRI extension)
54EC (bonds) and 54EE (startup fund)

questions.explanation: Remove 54EE and 54GB from your templates. 54EE was a dead letter (specified fund never notified). 54GB expired 31 March 2025. All other exemptions (54, 54B, 54D, 54EC, 54F, 54G, 54GA, 54H) continue with new section numbers.
questions.explanationWrong: Only 54EE and 54GB are removed. Every other exemption survives under a new number. 54G→87, 54GA→88 — both alive. 54D→84, 54H→89 — both alive. 54EC→85 — alive.

questions.question: A client who claimed Section 54F exemption (now Section 86) in AY 2024-25 has a 3-year lock-in on the new house. The lock-in expires in FY 2026-27. If they sell the house in FY 2026-27 (post-April 2026), what governs the claw-back?

options.text: The 2025 Act — Section 536(2)(h) applies because the violation occurs after 1 April 2026
The 1961 Act — the original claim was under old Section 54F
No claw-back — the lock-in is under the old Act
The client can choose whichever Act is more favourable

questions.explanation: FY 2026-27 is post-April 2026, so Section 536(2)(h) applies. Even though the original exemption was under old Section 54F, the lock-in violation in the 2025 Act period triggers claw-back under 2025 Act provisions.
questions.explanationWrong: The violation date is what matters. Post-April 2026 = 2025 Act governs via Section 536(2)(h). No choice between Acts. The original claiming Act is irrelevant for determining the claw-back rules.

questions.question: Final synthesis: How many genuine 2025 Act changes exist in the exemptions chapter (Unit 7.3)?

options.text: Four: 54EE dropped, 54GB dropped, CGAS electronic deposits, Section 536(2)(h) claw-back transition
Zero — everything is pure renumbering
Ten — every section has some modification
Two — only the dropped exemptions

questions.explanation: Four and only four genuine 2025 Act changes: (1) 54EE not re-enacted, (2) 54GB not re-enacted, (3) CGAS accepts electronic deposits, (4) Section 536(2)(h) claw-back for post-April 2026 lock-in violations. Everything else in this chapter is renumbering or prior Finance Act changes carried forward.
questions.explanationWrong: The full count is four. Two dropped exemptions + CGAS digital upgrade + Section 536(2)(h). Don't forget the CGAS and claw-back provisions — they are genuine 2025 Act additions beyond the two dropped sections.
